subject: Physics
q: A alternating current at any instant is given by $i=[6+\sqrt{56} \sin (100 \pi t+\pi / 3)]$ A. The $r m s$ value of the current is ________ A.
question: A alternating current at any instant is given by $i=[6+\sqrt{56} \sin (100 \pi t+\pi / 3)]$ A. The $r m s$ value of the current is ________ A.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & I_{D N s}=\sqrt{\frac{\int i^2 d t}{\int d t}} \\ & I_{D n s}=\sqrt{(6)^2+\frac{(\sqrt{56})^2}{2}} \\ & =\sqrt{36+28} \\ & =\sqrt{64} \\ & =8 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
As per the given graph, choose the correct representation for curve A and curve B
{Where XC= Reactance of pure capacitive circuit connected with A.C. source
XL= Reactance of pure inductive circuit connected with A.C. source
R= Impedance of pure resistive circuit connected with A.C. source
Z= Impedance of the LCR series circuit }
question:
As per the given graph, choose the correct representation for curve A and curve B
{Where XC= Reactance of pure capacitive circuit connected with A.C. source
XL= Reactance of pure inductive circuit connected with A.C. source
R= Impedance of pure resistive circuit connected with A.C. source
Z= Impedance of the LCR series circuit }
solution:
The formula to calculate the inductive reactance is

XL=ωL   ...1

The formula to calculate capacitive reactance is

XC=1ωC   ...2

As can be seen from equation (1), the inductive reactance varies directly with the frequency and equation (2) indicates that capacitive reactance varies inversely with the frequency.

Hence, curve A represents the capacitive reactance and curve B represents the inductive reactance.

q:
A series LCR circuit driven by 300 V at a frequency of 50 Hz contains a resistance R=3 kΩ, an inductor of inductive reactance XL=250π Ω and an unknown capacitor. The value of capacitance to maximise the average power should be:
take π2=10
question:
A series LCR circuit driven by 300 V at a frequency of 50 Hz contains a resistance R=3 kΩ, an inductor of inductive reactance XL=250π Ω and an unknown capacitor. The value of capacitance to maximise the average power should be:
take π2=10
solution:
Angular frequency, ω=2πf=2π×50=100π rad s-1

At resonance,

XC=XL⇒1ωC=ωL⇒1ωC=250π⇒C=1100π×250π F=4 μF

subject: Physics
q:
An LCR circuit is equivalent to a damped pendulum. In an LCR circuit the capacitor is charged to Q0 and then connected to the L and R as shown below:

If a student plots graphs of the square of maximum charge QMax2 on the capacitor with time (t) for two different values L1 and L2(L1>L2) of L then which of the following represents this graph correctly? (plots are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question:
An LCR circuit is equivalent to a damped pendulum. In an LCR circuit the capacitor is charged to Q0 and then connected to the L and R as shown below:

If a student plots graphs of the square of maximum charge QMax2 on the capacitor with time (t) for two different values L1 and L2(L1>L2) of L then which of the following represents this graph correctly? (plots are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
Comparing to the damped pendulum. We write, mdvdt=-kx-bv, bv is resistive force, ⇒  Amplitude, A=A0e-bt2m. Comparing results, we can write

qc=+Ldidt+iR, as charge decreasing, 


qc=Ld2qdt2-dqdtR

⇒A=q; R=b, m=L
⇒q=q0e-Rt2L⇒q2=q02e-RtL⇒q2=q02e-RtL.

Exponentially decreasing function, L1 graph has higher values than L2.

subject: Physics
q: An alternating current is represented by the equation, $i=100 \sqrt{2} \sin (100 \pi t)$ ampere. The RMS value of current and the frequency of the given alternating current are
question: An alternating current is represented by the equation, $i=100 \sqrt{2} \sin (100 \pi t)$ ampere. The RMS value of current and the frequency of the given alternating current are
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_0}{\sqrt{2}}=100 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{f}=\frac{\mathrm{w}}{2 \pi}=\frac{100 \pi}{2 \pi}=50 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A coil of negligible resistance is connected in series with $90 \Omega$ resistor across $120 \mathrm{~V}, 60 \mathrm{~Hz}$ supply. A voltmeter reads $36 \mathrm{~V}$ across resistance. Inductance of the coil is :
question: A coil of negligible resistance is connected in series with $90 \Omega$ resistor across $120 \mathrm{~V}, 60 \mathrm{~Hz}$ supply. A voltmeter reads $36 \mathrm{~V}$ across resistance. Inductance of the coil is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 36=I_{\mathrm{rms}} \mathrm{R} \\ & 36=\frac{120}{\sqrt{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2+\mathrm{R}^2}} \times \mathrm{R} \\ & \mathrm{R}=90 \Omega \Rightarrow 36=\frac{120 \times 90}{\sqrt{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2+90^2}} \\ & \sqrt{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2+90^2}=300 \\ & \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2=81900 \\ & \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=286.18 \\ & \omega \mathrm{L}=286.18 \\ & \mathrm{~L}=\frac{286.18}{376.8} \\ & \mathrm{~L}=0.76 \mathrm{H}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A coil of inductance 1 H and resistance 100 Ω is connected to a battery of 6 V. Determine approximately :

(a) The time elapsed before the current acquires half of its steady-state value
(b) The energy stored in the magnetic field associated with the coil at an instant 15 ms after the circuit is switched on. (Given ln2=0.693, e-32=0.25)
question:
A coil of inductance 1 H and resistance 100 Ω is connected to a battery of 6 V. Determine approximately :

(a) The time elapsed before the current acquires half of its steady-state value
(b) The energy stored in the magnetic field associated with the coil at an instant 15 ms after the circuit is switched on. (Given ln2=0.693, e-32=0.25)
solution:
Given circuit is R-L growth circuit

The current is the circuit is given by, 

i=ER1-e-tτ

For current to be half of the peak value,

i=E2R=ER1-e-tτ

Solving t=τln2

t=LRln2=11000.693=0.00693≃7 ms

At time t=15 ms,

i15 ms=ER1-e-1510

⇒i=61001-14=34×6100=0.045 A

⇒U=12Li2

by solving we get U=1 mJ.

q: In a series LCR circuit, the inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and the capacitive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power factor of the circuit is :
question: In a series LCR circuit, the inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and the capacitive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power factor of the circuit is :
solution:
We know that power factor is cosϕ,

cosϕ=RZ   ...1

Z=R2+XL-XC2   ...2

⇒Z=62+(10-4)2

⇒Z=62∣cosϕ=662

cosϕ=12

q: In a series LCR circuit, a resistor of $300 \Omega$, a capacitor of 25 nF and an inductor of 100 mH are used. For maximum current in the circuit, the angular frequency of the ac source is _____ $\times 10^4$ radians $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.
question: In a series LCR circuit, a resistor of $300 \Omega$, a capacitor of 25 nF and an inductor of 100 mH are used. For maximum current in the circuit, the angular frequency of the ac source is _____ $\times 10^4$ radians $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}} \\ & \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{25 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}} \\ & \omega=\frac{10^{+6}}{5 \times 10}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement-I: The reactance of an ac circuit is zero. It is possible that the circuit contains a capacitor and an inductor.
Statement-II: In ac circuit, the average power delivered by the source never becomes zero.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement-I: The reactance of an ac circuit is zero. It is possible that the circuit contains a capacitor and an inductor.
Statement-II: In ac circuit, the average power delivered by the source never becomes zero.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
solution:
The reactance of a circuit containing a capacitor and an inductor is given by X=XL-XC=ωL-1ωC. An AC circuit has zero reactance at resonance, i.e., when, XL=XC. So, first statement is true.

The power consumption in an AC circuit is zero when circuit contains only inductance or capacitance since average power consumed in a cycle is zero for inductance or capacitance. So, second statement is false.

subject: Physics
q: A series $\mathrm{LR}$ circuit is connected to an ac source of frequency $\omega$ and the inductive reactance is equal to $2 \mathrm{R}$. A capacitance of capacitive reactance equal to $\mathrm{R}$ is added in series with $\mathrm{L}$ and $\mathrm{R}$. The ratio of the new power factor to the old one is :
question: A series $\mathrm{LR}$ circuit is connected to an ac source of frequency $\omega$ and the inductive reactance is equal to $2 \mathrm{R}$. A capacitance of capacitive reactance equal to $\mathrm{R}$ is added in series with $\mathrm{L}$ and $\mathrm{R}$. The ratio of the new power factor to the old one is :
solution:
Power factor ${ }_{\text {(old) }}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}{ }^2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+(2 \mathrm{R})^2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{5} \mathrm{R}}$
Power factor ${ }_{\text {(new) }}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+(2 R-R)^2}}$
$=\frac{R}{\sqrt{2} R}$
$\therefore \frac{\text { New power factor }}{\text { Old power factor }}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}}}{\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{5} \mathrm{R}}}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}}$

subject: Physics
q: An AC source is connected to an inductance of 100 mH, a capacitance of 100 μF and a resistance of 120 Ω as shown in figure. The time in which the resistance having a thermal capacity 2 J C-1∘ will get heated by 16°C is _____ s.
question: An AC source is connected to an inductance of 100 mH, a capacitance of 100 μF and a resistance of 120 Ω as shown in figure. The time in which the resistance having a thermal capacity 2 J C-1∘ will get heated by 16°C is _____ s.
solution:
From the diagram given,

Angular frequency ω=100 rad s-1 and Vrms=V02=2022=20 V.

Now, XL=ωL=100×100×10-3=10 Ω and XC=1ωC=1100×100×10-6=100 Ω

XL-XC=10-100=90 Ω

The impedance of the circuit,

Z=XL-XC2+R2=902+1202=150 Ω

The current in the circuit,

imrs=VrmsZ=20150 A 

Power factor cosϕ=RZ

Now

VrmsirmscosϕΔt=msΔT⇒Δt=msΔTVrms×VrmsZ×RZ=msΔTZ2Vrms2×R=2×16×22500400×120=15 s

⇒Δt=15 s

question:

solution:

subject: Physics
q: When a $d c$ voltage of $100 \mathrm{~V}$ is applied to an inductor, a $d c$ current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$ flows through it. When an ac voltage of $200 \mathrm{~V}$ peak value is connected to inductor, its inductive reactance is found to be $20 \sqrt{3} \Omega$. The power dissipated in the circuit is _______ W.
question: When a $d c$ voltage of $100 \mathrm{~V}$ is applied to an inductor, a $d c$ current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$ flows through it. When an ac voltage of $200 \mathrm{~V}$ peak value is connected to inductor, its inductive reactance is found to be $20 \sqrt{3} \Omega$. The power dissipated in the circuit is _______ W.
solution:
For DC voltage
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{100}{5}=20 \Omega$
for AC voltage
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=20 \sqrt{3} \Omega \\
& \mathrm{R}=20 \Omega \\
& \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2+\mathrm{R}^2}=\sqrt{3 \times 400+400}=40 \Omega \\
& \text { Power }=\mathrm{i}_{\text {rms }}^2 \mathrm{R} \\
& \quad=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {rms }}}{\mathrm{Z}}\right)^2 \times \mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{\frac{200}{\sqrt{2}}}{40}\right)^2 \times 20=250 \mathrm{~W}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit is connected to a voltage source with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> . After a very large time, current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> behaves as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>≫</mo><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>:
question: A series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit is connected to a voltage source with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> . After a very large time, current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> behaves as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>≫</mo><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>:
solution:
When sinusoidal voltage is applied across LR circuit, current is also sinusoidal but it lags by some phase.

Current will be in the form of
I=I0sinωt-ϕ
Graph will be sinusoidal.

Here I0 is the maximum current and ϕ is phase difference between V & I

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> and it is connected to ac voltage of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>m</mi></msub></mfenced><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. At frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math> lower than resonant frequency, the amplitude of current will be approximately
question: A series LCR circuit has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> and it is connected to ac voltage of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>m</mi></msub></mfenced><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. At frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math> lower than resonant frequency, the amplitude of current will be approximately
solution:
For an LCR circuit. the resonant angular frequency is given by, ω0=1LC=104 rad s-1

The given frequency is 60% lower than resonant frequency. Therefore,

ω'=0.4×104=4000 rad s-1

Reactance of the capacitor at given frequency,

XC=ω'C-1=250 Ω.

Reactance of the inductor at given frequency,

XL=ω'L=40 Ω

Now the amplitude of the current in the given circuit will be,

i0=V0R2+XC'-XL'2=50102+250-402=238 mA

subject: Physics
q: When you walk through a metal detector carrying a metal object in your pocket, it raises an alarm. This phenomenon works on
question: When you walk through a metal detector carrying a metal object in your pocket, it raises an alarm. This phenomenon works on
solution:
Metal detector works on the principle of transmitting an electromagnetic signal and analyses a return signal from the target. So it works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit.

When the LC circuit detects any resonating frequency from any metal which is near to it, electric field will be created which will lead to induces current in the coil and changes in the signal flow through the coil.

subject: Physics
q: In an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>A.C.</mtext></math> circuit, the instantaneous e.m.f. and current are given by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>30</mn><mi>t</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>30</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. In one cycle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>A.C.</mtext></math>, the average power consumed by the circuit (in watt) and the watt-less current (in ampere) are, respectively:
question: In an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>A.C.</mtext></math> circuit, the instantaneous e.m.f. and current are given by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>30</mn><mi>t</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>30</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. In one cycle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>A.C.</mtext></math>, the average power consumed by the circuit (in watt) and the watt-less current (in ampere) are, respectively:
solution:
Instantaneous emf and current is given by, E=100sin30t, I=20sin30t-π4.

Phase difference between emf and current is 

ϕ=30t-30t-π4=π4

Average power is given by,

 Pav=ErmsIrmscosϕ
=1002×202×12=10002



Watt-less current is given by, 

IWL=Irmssinϕ=Io√2sin45°⇒IWL=202.12=10 A.

question: A resistance $R$ and a capacitance $C$ are connected in series to a battery of negligible internal resistance through a key. The key is closed at $t=$ 0 . If after $t$ sec the voltage across the capacitance was seven times the voltage across $\mathrm{R}$, the value of $\mathrm{t}$ is
solution: $t=3 R C \ln 2$

subject: Physics
q:
For a series LCR circuit, I vs ω curve is shown 

(a) To the left of ωr, the circuit is mainly capacitive.
(b) To the left of ωr, the circuit is mainly inductive.
(c) At ωr, impedance of the circuit is equal to the resistance of the circuit.
(d) At ωr, impedance of the circuit is 0.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
For a series LCR circuit, I vs ω curve is shown 

(a) To the left of ωr, the circuit is mainly capacitive.
(b) To the left of ωr, the circuit is mainly inductive.
(c) At ωr, impedance of the circuit is equal to the resistance of the circuit.
(d) At ωr, impedance of the circuit is 0.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
solution:
The impedance of an AC circuit is given by, Z=R2+XC-XL2.

For current to be maximum Z should be minimum. The value of Z will be minimum when XC-XL=0. Therefore, the value of Z=R. Hence, statement C is correct and statement D is wrong.

To the left of ωr i.e. when ω<ωr, XC>XL. Therefore, the circuit is mainly capacitive. Hence, statement A is correct and statement B is wrong.

subject: Physics
q: In a series LCR circuit $R=200 \Omega$ and the voltage and the frequency of the main supply is $220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ respectively. On taking out the capacitance from the circuit the current lags behind the voltage by $30^{\circ}$. On taking out the inductor from the circuit the current leads the voltage by $30^{\circ}$. The power dissipated in the LCR circuit is
question: In a series LCR circuit $R=200 \Omega$ and the voltage and the frequency of the main supply is $220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ respectively. On taking out the capacitance from the circuit the current lags behind the voltage by $30^{\circ}$. On taking out the inductor from the circuit the current leads the voltage by $30^{\circ}$. The power dissipated in the LCR circuit is
solution:
The given circuit is under resonance as $X_L=X_C$ Hence power dissipated in the circuit is
$$
\mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{R}}=242 \mathrm{~W}
$$

question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance, a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitor and an inductor are connected in series across a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply at variable frequency. Calculate the value of inductance of inductor at which resonance will occur. Given that the resonant frequency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
solution:
C=0.1μF=10-7 F

Resonant frequency =60 Hz

ωo=1LC

2πf0=1LC⇒L=14π2f02C

by putting values L≃70.3 Hz.

subject: Physics
q: To light, a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> lamp is connected, in series with a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>50</mn><mrow><mi>π</mi><msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo> </mo><mi>AC</mi></math> source. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: To light, a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> lamp is connected, in series with a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>50</mn><mrow><mi>π</mi><msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo> </mo><mi>AC</mi></math> source. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
Electrical power is given by P=V2R⇒R=V2P

Resistance of lamp is R=100×10050⇒R=200 Ω

Let VC and VR be the voltage across capacitor and resistor.

Here, VR2+VC2=V2

Current through lamp is i=100200=12 A

⇒1002+VC2=2002

 ⇒VC2=30000⇒VC=1003 V

We know that, V=i×XC 

Then,  XC=2003 Ω=1ωC

So, capacitance of capacitor is C=120×50×203=50×10-6x

x=50×10-6×100×2003

Thus, value of x=3.

question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is connected to a resistor. solution:
i=i0cos(ωt)
i=i0 at t=0

t=π4ω=π4(2πf)=18f

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal voltage $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=100 \sin (500 \mathrm{t})$ is applied across a pure inductance of $L=0.02 \mathrm{H}$. The current through the coil is:
question: A sinusoidal voltage $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=100 \sin (500 \mathrm{t})$ is applied across a pure inductance of $L=0.02 \mathrm{H}$. The current through the coil is:
solution:
In a pure inductive circuit current always lags behind the emf by $\frac{\pi}{2}$.
If $\mathrm{v}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{v}_0 \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$ then $I=I_0 \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ Now, given $v(t)=100 \sin (500 t)$ and $\mathrm{I}_0=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{\omega \mathrm{L}}=\frac{100}{500 \times 0.02}[\because \mathrm{L}=0.02 \mathrm{H}]$ $\mathrm{I}_0=10 \sin \left(500 \mathrm{t}-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ $\mathrm{I}_0=-10 \cos (500 \mathrm{t})$

q: A transformer consisting of <math><mn>300</mn></math> turns in the primary and <math><mn>150</mn></math> turns in the secondary gives output power of <math><mi> </mi><mn>2.2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> . If the current in the secondary coil is <math><mi> </mi><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> , then the input voltage and current in the primary coil are:
question: A transformer consisting of <math><mn>300</mn></math> turns in the primary and <math><mn>150</mn></math> turns in the secondary gives output power of <math><mi> </mi><mn>2.2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> . If the current in the secondary coil is <math><mi> </mi><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> , then the input voltage and current in the primary coil are:
solution:
Assuming 100% efficiency,

V2V1=i1i2=N2N1

i1i2=N2N1i110=150300i1=5 A

P=VI2200=5×V1V1=440 V

q: A capacitor of capacitance 150.0 μF is connected to an alternating source of emf given by E=36 sin120πt V. The maximum value of current in the circuit is approximately equal to:
question: A capacitor of capacitance 150.0 μF is connected to an alternating source of emf given by E=36 sin120πt V. The maximum value of current in the circuit is approximately equal to:
solution:
The formula for maximum current is given by 

Imax=ωCVm   ...(i)

The given data is 

ω=120π

Vm=36 VC=150 μF

Substituting the values in equation (i)

Imax=120π×150×10-6×36

=2.036 A

≈2 A

subject: Physics
q: For the LCR circuit, shown here, the current is observed to lead the applied voltage. An additional capacitor C′ , when joined with the capacitor C present in the circuit, makes the power factor of the circuit unity. The capacitor C′ , must have been connected in:
question: For the LCR circuit, shown here, the current is observed to lead the applied voltage. An additional capacitor C′ , when joined with the capacitor C present in the circuit, makes the power factor of the circuit unity. The capacitor C′ , must have been connected in:
options: Parallel with C and has a magnitude <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

Series with C and has a magnitude  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

Series with C and has a magnitude  <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math>

Parallel with C and has a magnitude  <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math>
solution:
Current is leading,  it means inductive reactance is more than capacitive reactance

For making power factor to be unity the value of C is to be increased.

⇒ another capacitor C′ should be connected in parallel. So in that condition XL=XC

⇒ωL=1ωCeq⇒Ceq=1ω2L

⇒C+C′=1ω2L⇒C′=1ω2L-C

⇒C′=1-ω2LCω2L

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the magnitude of VL and VC are twice that of VR. Given that f=50 Hz, the inductance of the coil is 1 Kπ mH. The value of K is _____ .
question: In the given circuit, the magnitude of VL and VC are twice that of VR. Given that f=50 Hz, the inductance of the coil is 1 Kπ mH. The value of K is _____ .
solution:
Since the voltage across the inductor and capacitor is same, the circuit is in resonance. Therefore, the current in the circuit will be,

i=VR=2205=44 A.

The voltage across the inductor is double that across the resistor. Therefore, VL=2VR=440 V.

We know that VL=iXL=i×2πfL⇒L=44044×100π=110π H=100πmH

Therefore, K=1100=0.01≈0.

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of self inductance 1 H connected in series with a resistor of $100 \pi \mathrm{ohm}$ and an ac supply of $100 \pi$ volt, 50 Hz. Maximum current flowing in the circuit is ________ A.
question: An inductor of self inductance 1 H connected in series with a resistor of $100 \pi \mathrm{ohm}$ and an ac supply of $100 \pi$ volt, 50 Hz. Maximum current flowing in the circuit is ________ A.
solution:
Impedance of circuit
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\omega_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(100 \pi)^2+(2 \pi \times 50 \times 1)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(100 \pi)^2+(100 \pi)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{2} \times 100 \pi \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{V}{2}=\frac{100 \pi}{\sqrt{2} \times 100 \pi}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\max }=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \text { Ampere }
\end{aligned}$
Correct Answer : 1
